slides.title: Based on the IEEE 802.11i standard.
lines: The primary enhancement over WPA is the use of the
The final form of the 802.11i standard is RSN (Robust Security Network)
slides.text:
Based on the IEEE 802.11i standard.
The primary enhancement over WPA is the use of the
AES-based CCMP
Wi-Fi Protected Access 2 (WPA2)
The final form of the 802.11i standard is RSN (Robust Security Network)

slides.title: The Personal mode uses a PSK (Pre-Shared Key) and doesn't require a separate authentication of users.
lines: The Personal mode uses a PSK (Pre-Shared Key) and doesn't require a separate authentication of users.
The Enterprise mode requires the users to be separately authenticated by using the Extended EAP (Extensible Authentication Protocol).
slides.text:
The Personal mode uses a PSK (Pre-Shared Key) and doesn't require a separate authentication of users.
The Enterprise mode requires the users to be separately authenticated by using the Extended EAP (Extensible Authentication Protocol).
Wi-Fi Protected Access 2 (WPA2)
WPA2 Versions
Personal
Enterprise

Phase 1: Discovery

slides.title: An AP uses messages called Beacons and Probe Responses to advertise its security capabilities
lines: An AP uses messages called Beacons and Probe Responses to advertise its security capabilities
Advertised in an RSN Information Element (RSN IE)
The STA uses these to identify an AP for a WLAN with which it wishes to communicate
The STA associates with the AP, which it uses to select the cipher suite and authentication mechanism in Association Request
slides.text:
An AP uses messages called Beacons and Probe Responses to advertise its security capabilities
Advertised in an RSN Information Element (RSN IE)
The STA uses these to identify an AP for a WLAN with which it wishes to communicate
The STA associates with the AP, which it uses to select the cipher suite and authentication mechanism in Association Request
Capability Discovery

slides.title: The purpose of this frame sequence, is simply to maintain backward compatibility with the IEEE 802.11 state machine
lines: The purpose of this frame sequence, is simply to maintain backward compatibility with the IEEE 802.11 state machine
In essence, the two devices (STA and AP) simply exchange identifiers
slides.text:
The purpose of this frame sequence, is simply to maintain backward compatibility with the IEEE 802.11 state machine
It provides no security
In essence, the two devices (STA and AP) simply exchange identifiers
Open System Authentication

slides.title: The purpose of this stage is to agree on a set of security capabilities to be used
lines: The purpose of this stage is to agree on a set of security capabilities to be used
The STA then sends an Association Request frame to the AP.
Specify one set of matching capabilities (one authentication and key management suite, one pairwise cipher suite, and one group-key cipher suite) from among those advertised by the AP
slides.text:
The purpose of this stage is to agree on a set of security capabilities to be used
The STA then sends an Association Request frame to the AP.
Specify one set of matching capabilities (one authentication and key management suite, one pairwise cipher suite, and one group-key cipher suite) from among those advertised by the AP
Association

Connect to AS
The STA sends a request to its AP that it has an association with for connection to the AS;
The AP acknowledges this request and sends an access request to the AS
EAP exchange
Authenticate the STA and AS to each other
Secure key delivery

master session key

Authentication Steps

slides.title: Starts the 802.11X exchange with EAPOL-start message
lines: Starts the 802.11X exchange with EAPOL-start message
Supplicant replies with EAP-response/identity frame and passed to the radius server
Radius server determines the type of authentication required and sends an EAP-request for particular method type
The supplicant replies with an EAP-response/method frame
Steps 4 and 5 are repeated to complete the authentication
The radius server grants request with Radius-access-accept packet
slides.text:
Starts the 802.11X exchange with EAPOL-start message
Authenticator issues an EAP-request/identity frame
Supplicant replies with EAP-response/identity frame and passed to the radius server
Radius server determines the type of authentication required and sends an EAP-request for particular method type
The supplicant replies with an EAP-response/method frame
Steps 4 and 5 are repeated to complete the authentication
The radius server grants request with Radius-access-accept packet
EAP Exchange Process

slides.title: PMK – Pairwise Master Key
lines: Sent from the AS to the Authenticator
Both the Supplicant and Authenticator now have the same PMK
PMK is permanent for the entire session
Must generate a Pairwise Transient Key for encryption of data.
slides.text:
PMK – Pairwise Master Key
Sent from the AS to the Authenticator
Both the Supplicant and Authenticator now have the same PMK
PMK is permanent for the entire session
Must generate a Pairwise Transient Key for encryption of data.
Done using
4-way handshake
Phase 3 Key Management

slides.title: Confirm that the client holds the PMK.
lines: Confirm that the client holds the PMK.
Confirm that the PMK is correct and up-to-date.
Create pairwise transient key (PTK) from the PMK.
Install the pairwise encryption and integrity keys into IEEE 802.11.
Transport the group temporal key (GTK) and GTK sequence number from Authenticator to Supplicant and install the GTK and GTK sequence number in the STA and, if not already installed, in the AP.
slides.text:
Confirm that the client holds the PMK.
Confirm that the PMK is correct and up-to-date.
Create pairwise transient key (PTK) from the PMK.
Install the pairwise encryption and integrity keys into IEEE 802.11.
Transport the group temporal key (GTK) and GTK sequence number from Authenticator to Supplicant and install the GTK and GTK sequence number in the STA and, if not already installed, in the AP.
Confirm the cipher suite selection.
Four-Way Handshake

, then the PMK will be equal to PSK.
, then the PMK will be derived from the 802.1X authentication MK.

lines: Used to compute MIC on WPA EAPOL Key message
AP uses this key to encrypt additional data sent (in the 'Key Data' field) to the client (for example, the RSN IE or the GTK)
Used to encrypt/decrypt Unicast data packets

Used to compute MIC on WPA EAPOL Key message
16 bytes of EAPOL-Key Encryption Key (KEK)
AP uses this key to encrypt additional data sent (in the 'Key Data' field) to the client (for example, the RSN IE or the GTK)
16 bytes of Temporal Key (TK)
Used to encrypt/decrypt Unicast data packets
64 bytes Pairwise Transient Key

slides.title: The primary enhancement over WPA is the use of the AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) algorithm.
lines: The primary enhancement over WPA is the use of the AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) algorithm.
slides.text:
The primary enhancement over WPA is the use of the AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) algorithm.
128 bit AES using the
Counter-Mode/CBC-MAC Protocol
(CCMP)
WPA2 Encryption
CCMP
CTR
CBC-MAC

slides.title: The encryption service in WPA2 is based on the AES algorithm used in the CTR mode.
lines: The encryption service in WPA2 is based on the AES algorithm used in the CTR mode.
It generates the next keystream block by encrypting successive values of a "counter"
slides.text:
The encryption service in WPA2 is based on the AES algorithm used in the CTR mode.
It generates the next keystream block by encrypting successive values of a "counter"
Counter Mode Encryption

slides.title: In WPA2, the data integrity check is carried out by computing the CBC-MAC message authentication code
lines: In WPA2, the data integrity check is carried out by computing the CBC-MAC message authentication code
XOR each 128- bit block of the data with the AES encrypted value as obtained from the previous 128-bit block
slides.text:
In WPA2, the data integrity check is carried out by computing the CBC-MAC message authentication code
XOR each 128- bit block of the data with the AES encrypted value as obtained from the previous 128-bit block
Cipher Block Chaining (CBC) - MAC

slides.title: Counter-Mode/CBC-MAC Protocol (CCMP)
slides.text: Counter-Mode/CBC-MAC Protocol (CCMP)

slides.title: CCMP provides integrity protection over both the frame body and nearly the entire header in a MAC frame,
lines: CCMP provides integrity protection over both the frame body and nearly the entire header in a MAC frame,
which prevents an adversary from exploiting the MAC headers
to prevent replay attacks and construct a fresh nonce for each packet
Analysis suggests that once CCMP is implemented,
an adversary will not able to break the data confidentiality and integrity without the knowledge of the key
slides.text:
CCMP provides integrity protection over both the frame body and nearly the entire header in a MAC frame,
which prevents an adversary from exploiting the MAC headers
CCMP uses a 48-bit Packet Number (PN)
to prevent replay attacks and construct a fresh nonce for each packet
Analysis suggests that once CCMP is implemented,
an adversary will not able to break the data confidentiality and integrity without the knowledge of the key
AES – CCMP Features

slides.title: The WPA2 has immunity against many types of hacker attacks, like:
lines: The WPA2 has immunity against many types of hacker attacks, like:
slides.text:
The WPA2 has immunity against many types of hacker attacks, like:
Man-in-the-middle.
Authentication forging.
Replay.
Key collision.
Weak keys.
Packet forging.
Brute–force/dictionary attacks.
WPA2 Pros

slides.title: WPA2 adds two enhancements to support fast roaming as follow:
lines: WPA2 adds two enhancements to support fast roaming as follow:
Allow the client to reconnect to the access points which he has recently been connected to without needing to re-authenticate because of the PMK caching feature.
Allow the client to pre-authenticate himself with the access point which he is moving toward while he is still connected to the access point which he is moving away from.
slides.text:
WPA2 adds two enhancements to support fast roaming as follow:
Allow the client to reconnect to the access points which he has recently been connected to without needing to re-authenticate because of the PMK caching feature.
Allow the client to pre-authenticate himself with the access point which he is moving toward while he is still connected to the access point which he is moving away from.
WPA2 Pros (cont.)

slides.title: Like all Wi-Fi security standard, the WPA2 can't stand in front of the physical layer attacks like:
lines: Like all Wi-Fi security standard, the WPA2 can't stand in front of the physical layer attacks like:
slides.text:
Like all Wi-Fi security standard, the WPA2 can't stand in front of the physical layer attacks like:
RF jamming.
Data flooding.
Access points failure.
WPA2 Cons

slides.title: The attacker can get and discover lots of network information by analyzing the unprotected control and management frames
lines: The attacker can get and discover lots of network information by analyzing the unprotected control and management frames.
It is vulnerable to the MAC addresses spoofing and the mass de-authentication attacks.
slides.text:
The attacker can get and discover lots of network information by analyzing the unprotected control and management frames.
It is vulnerable for the
DoS
attack.
It is vulnerable to the MAC addresses spoofing and the mass de-authentication attacks.
WPA2 Cons (cont.)

slides.title: KRACK is short for Key Reinstallation Attack
lines: KRACK is short for Key Reinstallation Attack
The attack uses vulnerabilities in the 4-way handshake of the current WPA- 2 protocol.
Threat level : Pretty bad – All Wi-Fi routers everywhere are vulnerable
slides.text:
KRACK is short for Key Reinstallation Attack
The attack uses vulnerabilities in the 4-way handshake of the current WPA- 2 protocol.
Threat level : Pretty bad – All Wi-Fi routers everywhere are vulnerable
The KRACK Attack

Negotiate fresh PTK: pairwise temporal key

Mutual authentication

slides.title: Limitations of formal proofs:
lines: WPA 4-way handshake has proven to be secure
Encryption ciphers have been mathematically proven to be secure
Enterprise PMK was just as vulnerable as Personal pre-shared keys.
Creates a false sense of security.
slides.text:
Limitations of formal proofs:
WPA 4-way handshake has proven to be secure
Encryption ciphers have been mathematically proven to be secure
Combination is not proven secure
Enterprise PMK was just as vulnerable as Personal pre-shared keys.
Creates a false sense of security.
Abstract model ≠ real code
Must assure code matches specification
Key Takeaway

full_text:
Lecture 11: Wi-Fi Protected Access 2 (WPA2)
Instructor:
Kui
Ren
Wireless network
and IoT security

Based on the IEEE 802.11i standard.
The primary enhancement over WPA is the use of the
AES-based CCMP
Wi-Fi Protected Access 2 (WPA2)
The final form of the 802.11i standard is RSN (Robust Security Network)

The Personal mode uses a PSK (Pre-Shared Key) and doesn't require a separate authentication of users.
The Enterprise mode requires the users to be separately authenticated by using the Extended EAP (Extensible Authentication Protocol).
Wi-Fi Protected Access 2 (WPA2)
WPA2 Versions
Personal
Enterprise

Phase 1: The AP and the client agree on the security policy
Phase 2: Generate the
master key
Phase 3: Creating
temporary keys
Phase 4: All keys generated in phase 3 will be used by the CCMP protocol to provide data
confidentiality
and
integrity
WPA2 Phases of Operation

Capability Discovery
Open System Authentication
Association
Phase 1: Discovery

An AP uses messages called Beacons and Probe Responses to advertise its security capabilities
Advertised in an RSN Information Element (RSN IE)
The STA uses these to identify an AP for a WLAN with which it wishes to communicate
The STA associates with the AP, which it uses to select the cipher suite and authentication mechanism in Association Request
Capability Discovery

The purpose of this frame sequence, is simply to maintain backward compatibility with the IEEE 802.11 state machine
It provides no security
In essence, the two devices (STA and AP) simply exchange identifiers
Open System Authentication

The purpose of this stage is to agree on a set of security capabilities to be used
The STA then sends an Association Request frame to the AP.
Specify one set of matching capabilities (one authentication and key management suite, one pairwise cipher suite, and one group-key cipher suite) from among those advertised by the AP
Association

This phase is based on EAP and the authentication method which has been agreed on in phase 1
Phase 2: Authentication

Connect to AS
The STA sends a request to its AP that it has an association with for connection to the AS;
The AP acknowledges this request and sends an access request to the AS
EAP exchange
Authenticate the STA and AS to each other
Secure key delivery
Once authentication is established, the AS generates a
master session key
and sends it to the STA
Authentication Steps

Starts the 802.11X exchange with EAPOL-start message
Authenticator issues an EAP-request/identity frame
Supplicant replies with EAP-response/identity frame and passed to the radius server
Radius server determines the type of authentication required and sends an EAP-request for particular method type
The supplicant replies with an EAP-response/method frame
Steps 4 and 5 are repeated to complete the authentication
The radius server grants request with Radius-access-accept packet
EAP Exchange Process

PMK – Pairwise Master Key
Sent from the AS to the Authenticator
Both the Supplicant and Authenticator now have the same PMK
PMK is permanent for the entire session
Must generate a Pairwise Transient Key for encryption of data.
Done using
4-way handshake
Phase 3 Key Management

Confirm that the client holds the PMK.
Confirm that the PMK is correct and up-to-date.
Create pairwise transient key (PTK) from the PMK.
Install the pairwise encryption and integrity keys into IEEE 802.11.
Transport the group temporal key (GTK) and GTK sequence number from Authenticator to Supplicant and install the GTK and GTK sequence number in the STA and, if not already installed, in the AP.
Confirm the cipher suite selection.
Four-Way Handshake

Four-Way Handshake

The PMK (Pairwise Master Key) derivation depends on what we have used on the authentication method
If we used
PSK (Pre-Shared Key)
, then the PMK will be equal to PSK.
If we used an
authentication server
, then the PMK will be derived from the 802.1X authentication MK.
Pairwise Key Hierarchies
Master Key (MK)
Pair-wise Master Key (PMK)
Pair-wise Transient Key (PTK)
Key Confirmation Key (KCK)
Key Encryption Key (KEK)
Temporal Key (TK)
Pre-Shared Key (PSK)

16 bytes of EAPOL-Key Confirmation Key (KCK)
Used to compute MIC on WPA EAPOL Key message
16 bytes of EAPOL-Key Encryption Key (KEK)
AP uses this key to encrypt additional data sent (in the 'Key Data' field) to the client (for example, the RSN IE or the GTK)
16 bytes of Temporal Key (TK)
Used to encrypt/decrypt Unicast data packets
64 bytes Pairwise Transient Key

The primary enhancement over WPA is the use of the AES (Advanced Encryption Standard) algorithm.
128 bit AES using the
Counter-Mode/CBC-MAC Protocol
(CCMP)
WPA2 Encryption
CCMP
CTR
CBC-MAC

The encryption service in WPA2 is based on the AES algorithm used in the CTR mode.
It generates the next keystream block by encrypting successive values of a "counter"
Counter Mode Encryption

In WPA2, the data integrity check is carried out by computing the CBC-MAC message authentication code
XOR each 128- bit block of the data with the AES encrypted value as obtained from the previous 128-bit block
Cipher Block Chaining (CBC) - MAC

Counter-Mode/CBC-MAC Protocol (CCMP)

CCMP provides integrity protection over both the frame body and nearly the entire header in a MAC frame,
which prevents an adversary from exploiting the MAC headers
CCMP uses a 48-bit Packet Number (PN)
to prevent replay attacks and construct a fresh nonce for each packet
Analysis suggests that once CCMP is implemented,
an adversary will not able to break the data confidentiality and integrity without the knowledge of the key
AES – CCMP Features

Encryption Method Comparison
WEP
WPA
WPA2
Cipher
RC4
RC4
AES
Key Sizes
40 bits
128bits encryption
64 bits authentication
128 bits
Key Life
24 bit IV
48 bit IV
48 bit IV
Packet Key
Concatenated
Mixing Function
Not Needed
Data Integrity
CRC-32
Michael Algorithm
CCM
Header Integrity
None
Michael Algorithm
CCM
Defeat
Replay Attacks
None
IV Sequence
IV Sequence
Key Management
None
EAP Based
EAP Based

The WPA2 has immunity against many types of hacker attacks, like:
Man-in-the-middle.
Authentication forging.
Replay.
Key collision.
Weak keys.
Packet forging.
Brute–force/dictionary attacks.
WPA2 Pros

WPA2 adds two enhancements to support fast roaming as follow:
Allow the client to reconnect to the access points which he has recently been connected to without needing to re-authenticate because of the PMK caching feature.
Allow the client to pre-authenticate himself with the access point which he is moving toward while he is still connected to the access point which he is moving away from.
WPA2 Pros (cont.)

Like all Wi-Fi security standard, the WPA2 can't stand in front of the physical layer attacks like:
RF jamming.
Data flooding.
Access points failure.
WPA2 Cons

The attacker can get and discover lots of network information by analyzing the unprotected control and management frames.
It is vulnerable for the
DoS
attack.
It is vulnerable to the MAC addresses spoofing and the mass de-authentication attacks.
WPA2 Cons (cont.)

KRACK is short for Key Reinstallation Attack
The attack uses vulnerabilities in the 4-way handshake of the current WPA- 2 protocol.
Threat level : Pretty bad – All Wi-Fi routers everywhere are vulnerable
The KRACK Attack

Used to connect to any protected Wi-Fi network
Two main purposes:
Mutual authentication
Negotiate fresh PTK: pairwise temporal key
Appeared to be secure:
No attacks in over a decade (apart from password guessing)
Proven that negotiated key (PTK) is secret
And encryption protocol proven secure
The 4-way handshake

4-way handshake (simplified)
PTK = Combine(shared secret,
ANonce
,
SNonce
)

4-way handshake (simplified)
PTK is installed

The Key Reinstallation Attack on WPA2

Frame encryption (simplified)
Nonce reuse implies keystream reuse (in all WPA2 ciphers)

4-way handshake (simplified)
Installing PTK initializes nonce to zero

Reinstallation Attack

Reinstallation Attack
Block MSG4

Reinstallation Attack
Because PTK has been installed
Msg4 is now encrypted

Reinstallation Attack
Key Reinstallation!
Nonce is reset

Reinstallation Attack
Same nonce is used

Reinstallation Attack
Keystream
Decrypted Data

Limitations of formal proofs:
WPA 4-way handshake has proven to be secure
Encryption ciphers have been mathematically proven to be secure
Combination is not proven secure
Enterprise PMK was just as vulnerable as Personal pre-shared keys.
Creates a false sense of security.
Abstract model ≠ real code
Must assure code matches specification
Key Takeaway
